summary: Cantrill reflects on how his father's career as an emergency physician influenced his approach to software engineering, specifically through two medical practices: Morbidity and Mortality meetings (rigorous post-failure analysis) and Journal Club (regular discussion of research papers). He describes implementing both at Sun and Joyent, noting that while engineers tend to prefer forward-looking discussions over post-mortems, both practices improve engineering culture. The post culminates in mentioning his father's article on healthcare computing for ACM Queue.
key_points: M&M meetings in medicine provide a model for rigorous failure analysis that engineering organizations should adopt
Journal Club helps bridge research and practice, fostering cross-organizational communication
Engineers naturally prefer forward-looking discussions over examining past failures
Medicine and software engineering are at opposite ends of a spectrum: deterministic/brittle vs sloppy/robust
Cantrill was introduced to computing through his physician father who wrote software for his emergency department
money_quotes: The reality is that if we software engineers screw up, people don't generally die.
Not only are the consequences less dire, but the underlying systems themselves are at opposite ends of a kind of spectrum: one is deterministic yet brittle, the other sloppy yet robust.

The purpose of this meeting is to learn from failure, and physicians in M&M are brutally honest with themselves and their peers to determine what happened and why – and if any other outcome was possible.
I think many software engineering organizations would stand to benefit by examining what went so horribly wrong and why.

For my sister and me, Journal Club at our house was a kind of annual holiday, for it represented the one sanctioned vector by which soda pop (an otherwise forbidden nectar) found itself in the fridge – seemingly by the ton.
key_insight: Software engineering organizations should adopt medicine's disciplined practices of post-failure analysis and research discussion to build better engineering culture and learn from systemic failures.
